title: Worming and parasite control in chickens
subtitle: When to worm, which licensed products to use in the UK, and how to reduce worm burden in the environment
excerpt: Intestinal worms are common in chickens kept on the same ground year after year. A routine worming programme using licensed products, combined with good management of the land, keeps worm burdens below the level that causes production loss and illness. This guide covers the main species, the UK-licensed treatment options, and the land management steps that make treatment more effective.
sourceNotes: Gail Damerow, The Chicken Health Handbook, 2nd ed. (2015), chapter on internal parasites. APHA, Poultry internal parasites guidance (UK, 2022). NOAH (National Office of Animal Health), UK veterinary product summaries for Flubenvet.

glossaryTerms.definition: Ascaridia galli, the most common internal parasite of chickens in the UK. Adult worms live in the small intestine and can grow to 12 cm long. Heavy burdens cause weight loss, diarrhoea, and reduced egg production. Eggs are passed in the droppings and persist in soil for years.

Syngamus trachea, a worm that lives in the trachea (windpipe) of chickens. It causes a characteristic stretching-and-shaking of the neck called gaping as the bird tries to clear its airway. Heavy infestations can cause death by suffocation. Earthworms and slugs carry the infective larvae.

A drug that kills or expels internal parasites. In the UK, the only licensed anthelmintic for chickens is flubendazole (sold as Flubenvet). It is a prescription-exempt product available from agricultural merchants and is administered in the feed.

content.text: Most chickens carry a low level of intestinal worms without showing obvious illness. Problems arise when worm burdens build up, usually on ground that has carried birds for several years. The main species to know are
roundworm
and
gapeworm
. Both are treated with the same
anthelmintic
in the UK.
Signs of a significant worm burden

A bird with a heavy worm burden may show weight loss, pale yolk colour, loose droppings, or a dull and fluffed-up look. Gapeworm causes a stretching neck and open-beak gasping. These signs are not unique to worms. They should prompt treatment and a vet check if they continue after treatment.
When to worm

There is no single correct answer for frequency. On fresh ground with a small flock, birds may never need routine worming. On land that has carried chickens for five or more years, treating twice a year is a reasonable baseline. The signs above are a trigger for treatment at any time of year.
Treat new birds before they join an existing flock. Treat the whole flock at the same time; leaving untreated birds in the same space undoes treatment for the birds you did treat.

attrs.title: Worm egg count testing
attrs.body: You can send a pooled droppings sample to a veterinary laboratory for a worm egg count test. This tells you whether your flock has a significant burden before you treat and allows you to confirm whether treatment was effective afterwards. It costs under twenty pounds for most postal services and removes the guesswork from your worming programme.

content.text: The licensed product in the UK: Flubenvet

Flubendazole, sold as Flubenvet 1% premixture, is the only licensed anthelmintic for laying hens in the UK. It is available from agricultural merchants and online without a prescription. It is mixed into the feed at the labelled inclusion rate and fed for seven consecutive days. The product licence covers roundworm, gapeworm, hairworm, and caecal worm.

There is a zero-day egg withdrawal period for Flubenvet when used at the licensed dose in laying hens. This means eggs can be eaten throughout the treatment course. Check the current product datasheet for the correct inclusion rate and the meat withdrawal period if you are treating birds destined for the table.

attrs.title: Unlicensed products
attrs.body: Several products marketed as natural wormers have no licensed efficacy evidence for poultry in the UK. Using an unlicensed product where a licensed one exists is not a legal route to treatment if the flock is producing eggs or meat for human consumption. Herbal supplements may be used as part of a management programme, but they are not a substitute for Flubenvet where treatment is needed.

content.text: Environmental management to reduce worm burden

Treatment alone does not solve a worm problem on heavily stocked land. Roundworm eggs can persist in soil for years. A few days after treatment the treated birds begin picking up new eggs from the same contaminated ground. Management of the land is the only long-term solution.
Rotate runs. If you have two or more run areas, alternate them each season. Resting ground for at least three months reduces the infective larval population on the surface.
Keep stocking density low. Worm burden on the ground is proportional to the number of birds and how long they have been there. Overstocking accelerates contamination.
Move the coop to fresh ground regularly if your setup allows. Even a move of a few metres breaks the cycle on heavily used areas.
Control earthworms in the run by improving drainage. Earthworms carry gapeworm larvae. Poorly drained, wet ground has a higher earthworm population and a higher gapeworm risk.

entries.cue: Birds look unwell again within four weeks of Flubenvet treatment
entries.cause: Rapid re-infestation from a heavily contaminated run; birds picking up large numbers of eggs immediately after treatment
entries.fix: The treatment worked but environmental burden is very high. Move birds to fresh ground after treatment, even temporarily. Consider a second treatment course after 21 days if signs return.

entries.cue: One bird shows gaping (stretching neck and open-beak gasping)
entries.cause: Gapeworm infestation in the trachea; can be rapid in onset if the bird ate infective earthworms
entries.fix: Treat the whole flock with Flubenvet immediately. Isolate the gaping bird to reduce stress and monitor closely. If the bird cannot breathe adequately, contact a vet promptly; a heavy gapeworm burden is life-threatening.

entries.cue: Flock on fresh ground with no prior birds, but worm egg count comes back positive
entries.cause: Ground was previously used by wild birds or game birds; or new birds were not treated on arrival and brought worms with them
entries.fix: Treat with Flubenvet for seven days. Review biosecurity on arrival of any new birds. Test again after treatment to confirm it worked.
